mappings.rationaleAI: Polar region encompassing indigenous peoples and their cultures across the eastern Arctic (A.1) and western Arctic (A.2), including Alaska, Canada, and Greenland.
Succession of indigenous societies along the Andes and Pacific coast of South America, from Norte Chico through the Inca, situated in the central and southern Andes (D.1).
Largest North American mountain system, with coverage of indigenous peoples' history across the Plateau (B.4), Great Basin (B.5), and Plains (B.7) regions.
Third-largest continent encompassing Paleo-Indian settlement and indigenous cultures across sub-Arctic (B.1), Northwest Coast (B.2), Plains (B.7), eastern woodlands (B.8), and other regions.
Smallest continent spanning Australia (I.1), Melanesia (I.2), and Polynesia (I.3), with Aboriginal, Maori, and Pacific Islander cultural traditions.
Largest pre-Columbian empire centered at Cusco spanning the western Andes, governing through Quechua, directly representing peoples of the central and southern Andes (D.1).
South American republic with pre-Columbian settlement, situated between Central America and the northern Andes (C.3) and the central and southern Andes (D.1).
Earth's largest hot desert covering most of North Africa, directly relevant to the peoples and cultures of the Maghreb (F.1).
Pacific subregion of over 1,000 islands with shared Austronesian linguistic and cultural traditions, directly constituting the peoples and cultures of Polynesia (I.3).
North Africa's largest country in the Maghreb, with Berber minority and Arabic-speaking majority, directly representing northwestern African peoples (F.1).
Transcontinental republic straddling Anatolia, directly representing the peoples and cultures of Turkey (F.4).
World's largest tropical forest covering the Amazon basin with over 30 million people of 350 ethnic groups, representing peoples of the tropical forest (D.2).
Longest continental mountain range forming the backbone of South America, with indigenous agriculture and cultural dimensions relevant to the central and southern Andes (D.1).
Bantu language spoken across East Africa and the Great Lakes region, reflecting the cultural and linguistic identity of peoples of central and lower East Africa (H.5).
Fourth-largest continent with indigenous, European, African, and Asian ancestry, encompassing Andean civilizations (D.1), tropical forest peoples (D.2), and nomadic groups (D.3).

African history from archaic humans through medieval empires and colonial penetration, covering sub-Saharan peoples across western Sudan (H.1), eastern Sudan (H.2), Guinea coast (H.3), Congo (H.4), East Africa (H.5), the Horn (H.6), and southern Africa (H.7).
Geocultural region comprising China, Japan, Korea, and Mongolia, directly representing the peoples and cultures of East Asia (G.3).
Northeast African country bridging Africa and West Asia, representing the peoples and cultures of the Mashriq (F.2).
West Africa's most populous country with Nok, Hausa, Benin, and Oyo cultural heritage, representing peoples of the Guinea coast (H.3).
Historical cultural region encompassing central and southern Mexico with Olmec, Maya, and Aztec civilizations, directly representing peoples of Mesoamerica (C.2).
All indigenous American civilizations before European contact, encompassing North American (B), Middle American (C), and South American (D) peoples.
Ethnic groups defined by cultural distinctiveness and ancestral territorial relationships across all world regions covered by this section.
Forced transportation of Africans covering origins and ethnic groups from sub-Saharan African peoples (H), affecting the demographic and cultural composition of multiple regions.
Earth's largest ocean flanking Oceania, with coverage of prehistoric Pacific migrations and island distribution relevant to peoples of Oceania (I).
